title: KMDS materials research data schema
description: working copy for version 2025-01; Materials Research Data Schema (version 2025-01) developed by the Expert Committee for Materials R&D Data Standardization in the National Center of Materials Research Data (NCMRD) of Korea

data name.description: name of the data set, named by data provider; This is your own unique data or specimen identifier. With this text string alone, you should be able to identify the materials (system) in your own internal data management system.
data name.examples: Zinc coated Fe-6Mn-3Al steel plate; Ti/Al dissimilar welded joint; Fe/C catalyst; Gd doped MOF 370; LLZO (Li7La3Zr2O12) Solid Electrolyte; Low Carbon TRIP Steel; 0.15C1.0Si1.5Mn1.5Al; Co single atom catalyst; Pt50Co50/TiO2; Material_1/Material_2/Material_3/Material_4;

data classification.description: classification code for relevant fields of science and technology announced by Ministry of Science and ICT of KOREA (https://www.law.go.kr)

data generation date.description: date when the data is added to the present database or data lake

data source.description: source information of the data, such as lab log, source publication, related projects, DOI of data, lead author, and publication date if the data is extracted from a publication; The DOI number referring to the published paper or dataset where the data can be found. If the data is unpublished, enter “Unpublished”; lead author is the surname of the first author. If several authors, end with et al. If the DOI number is given correctly, this will be extracted automatically from www.crossref.org ; publication date will be extracted automatically from www.crossref.org if the DOI number is given correctly.
data source.examples: extracted from Acta Mater vol 13, page 2089 (2000); archived on 01/01/2021; 10.1016/j.actamat.2016.08.081; generated in CNMD, POSTECH (2021)

name.description: contibutor's name in the form of "[last name], [first name]"

affiliation.description: contibutor's affliation (insitute's name and further description of working place)
affiliation.examples: Advanced Materials Research Center, KIST; Department of Energy, KAIST; Metallurgy Division, KIMS

researcher number.description: contributor’s researcher number ID in Integrated R&D Information System of Korea (https://iris.go.kr); only applied for Korean researchers

keywords.description: keywords representing the data set or material type and description, at least 3 keywords should be provided

embargo.description: date when allowing open access of the data

publication.description: information about the source publication; coexists with the existing free-form 'data source' field for backward compatibility. When DOI is provided here, bibliographic information can be auto-completed via Crossref ^-^

materials.description: inherent data of material that composed of the material system; this is array of n materials defined by the material_id

id.description: id of materials inside of the dataset, that is to be used for defining materials system

name.description: description of materials according to customary usage, designation system, composition, supplier, brand name, polymer name based on IUPAC recommendation (https://iupac.org/polymer-edu/polymer-naming/), etc.
name.examples: annealed INCONEL625 plate; Al7075-T6; Al10Si5Mg; Fe; CNT; TiO2; Ag; In2O2; LLZO; Aldrich CFO plate; poly(methyl methacrylate); poly[oxy(1-bromoethane-1,2-diyl)]; polypropylene; $$$ Toray T300; $$$ Owens Corning E-glass; $$$ Hyosung Carbon Fiber; Kukdo Epoxy $$$!@#; Hyosung Nylon $$$!@#; polyimide $$$!@#

doping.description: dopant and its dose for modifying structure and properties of materials

level.description: doping level as the dose of each element

dopant.description: name of dopant as element symbol

dose.description: dose of the element in material

constituent.description: name of constituent such as element symbol, molecular fomula, molecule name, etc

concentration.description: concentration of the element in material

unit.description: unit of the composition value in at.% or wt.%

density.description: mass per unit volum of a substance

cross-link density.description: number of cross-link bonds within a cross-linked polymer network &&&

hybrid bonds.description: fraction of s-p hybrid bond in the materials. This fraction is dependent on the materials process and is significant in understanding materials structure and properties in covalent bonded materials composed of C, N, O, B, Be, P, or S; **#

molecule.description: molecules as the unit of materials; the smallest structural unit of a polymer that retains the chemical compostion and connectivity of the repeating segments in the polymer chain; *** 신규 항목 설정

CAS number.description: a numeri(c)al designation assigned to chemical substances by the U.S. Chemical Abstracts Service (CAS)

chemical formula.description: the molecular formula about the chemical proportions of atoms that constitute whole number ratio of atoms present in a compound or constitutional unit

formula weight.description: sum of the atomic weight of each atom in the chemical formula

SMILES.description: human-readable string notation for describing the structure of molecules, simplified molecular-input line-entry system

structural formula.description: the molecular formula about a typographic system arose to describe organic structures in a line of functional group

plasticizer.description: a substance added to a material, typically a polymer, to make it softer, more flexible, and easier to handle. Plasticizers reduce intermolecular forces within the polymer matrix, decreasing the glass transition temperature and improving processability. &&&
plasticizer.examples: diethyl phthalates; dibutyl phthalate; citrate esters; natural oil

classification.description: 22 classifications based on the chemical structure of contituitional repeating unit of the polymer (Ref: https://polymer.nims.go.jp/PoLyInfo/guide/en/term_polymer.html)

degree of polymerization.description: number of each monomers contained in one polymer chain or polymer molecule; it is calculated for homopolymer by dividing the molecular weight of the polymer by the molecular weight of the repeat unit. ***

dispersity.description: ratio of the weight-average molecular weight to the number-average molecular weight; dispersity D = (M_w) / (M_n), where M_w is the weight-average molecular weight and M_n the number-average molecular weight
dispersity.alias: polydispersity index; PDI; molecular weight distribution

GPC (SEC) peak value.description: molecular weight obtained from the peak value refers to the maximum point of gel permeation chromatographic peak or size exclusion chromatrographic peak; the peak value provides the information about the molecular weight and distribution of polymers ***

number-average value.description: number-average molecular weights of materials; the average molecular weight of a polymer sample based on the number of molecules; M_n = sum{(N_i) x (M_i)} / sum {(N_i)}, where N_i is the number of molecules of species i and M_i the molecular weight of species i.

viscosity-average value.description: a measure to characterize the moleculer weight of a polymer based on its viscosity; particularly useful for understanding the flow behavior of polymer solutions and melts; M_v = K x [eta], where K is a constant that depends on the specific polymer and solvent used and [eta] the intrinsic viscosity of the polymer solution, which is a measure of how the viscosity of a solution changes with concentration. ***

weight-average value.description: weight-average molecular weights of materials; a measure of the average molecular weight of a polymer sample, taking into account the weight of each molecule; M_w = sum{(w_i) x (M_i)} / sum{(w_i)}, where w_i is the weight of species i and M_i the molecular weight of species i

z-average value.description: average molecular weight that gives greater weight to larger molecules, making ti paticularly sensitive to high molecular weight species within the distribution; M_z= sum{(M_i)^2 x (N_i)} / sum{(M_i) x (N_i)}, where M_i is the molecular weight of species i and N_i the number of molecules of species i *** &&&

architecture.description: molecular structure regarding atom connectivity and arragement; structural information when the polymer chain is seen within the range that is bigger than the constituitional repeating unit ***
architecture.alias: molecular structure; molecular arrangement; chain architecture

homogeneity.description: monomer homogeneity of the polymer chain

end groups.description: the chemical group or functional group present at the two ends of a polymer chain, which significantly influence the reactivity, properties and degradation of the polymer. ***

branch molecule.description: side chain that extend from the main polymer backbone chain; it is a deviation from a perfectly linear structure. ***
branch molecule.examples: LDPE; PP; PE; graft copolymers; PVC; PU

number of branches.description: average number of branches attached to a single poilymer molecule ***

degree of branching.description: percentage of the number of braches present in a polymer in its total number of repeating units ***

average branch length.description: length of the polymer chains between branching points ***

branching density.description: number of branches per unit length of the main polymer chain ***

branching index.description: the ratio between the radius of gyration of a branched polymer and that of a linear polymer of the same molar mass (range : 0~1) ***

crosslink density.description: The number of crosslinks per unit volume in a polymer network, representing the degree of interconnection between polymer chains through chemical bonds. It quantifies the three-dimensional network structure formed when polymer chains are linked together by covalent or ionic bonds. &&&
crosslink density.alias: network density; degree of crosslinking; crosslinking density; network crosslink concentration

crosslinking agent.description: A substance that promotes or regulates the formation of covalent bonds between polymer chains, resulting in a three-dimensional network structure. These agents react with functional groups on the polymer chains to create bridges or crosslinks that connect separate polymer molecules. &&&
crosslinking agent.alias: crosslinker; curing agent; hardener; vulcanizing agent; crosslinking additive; reticulating agent
crosslinking agent.examples: Sulfur (vulcanization of rubber); Dicumyl peroxide (crosslinking of polyethylene); Diisocyanates (polyurethane network formation); Benzoyl peroxide (free radical crosslinking of unsaturated polyesters); Glutaraldehyde (protein crosslinking); Epoxy hardeners (amine or anhydride-based curing agents); Azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN - photoinitiator for UV-curable systems); Trimethylolpropane triacrylate (TMPTA - radiation crosslinking); Metal salts (ionic crosslinking of alginates); Boric acid (PVA crosslinking)

core.description: central part of star polymer that acts as the central junction point from which all the polymer arms radiate outwards. ***

arm number.description: number of linear polymer chains that radiat from the central core ***

arm length.description: length of each individual polymer arm ***

number of generation.description: total number of generation to indicate the level of branching in a dendrimer. It starts from generation 0 (the core) and increases with each layer of branching. ***

core.description: central part of star polymer that acts as the central junction point from which all the polymer arms radiate outwards. ***

branches.definition: average number and length of branches in each generation ***

length.description: average length of branches in each generation ***

model.description: information of the computation model in the case of compuation or simulation data

box dimension.description: dimension parameters of the simulation box. Cubic simulation box is assumed if the angles are not defined.

phase.description: major state of the model or major crystal structure when the model is solid

structure file.description: URI of structure file used for compuation or simulation. File format is determined by extension of the file name

structure.description: structural information of materials in all scales; crystallographic, micro, meso and macro scales

crystallinity.description: the fraction (percentage) of a material that is crystalline (as opposed to amorhpous)

lattice parameter.description:
Lattice parameters of the lattice according to the convention described in web.archive.org. 
The unit cell is the basic building block of a crystal, repeated infinitely in three dimensions. It is characterised by:
* three vectors (a, b, c) that form the edges of a parallelepiped;
* the angles between the vectors (alpha, the angle between b and c; beta, the angle between a and c; gamma, the angle between a and b).
Depending on the crystal system, there are sometimes restrictions on the values that unit cell parameters can take.

unit cell.description: the smallest portion of a crystal lattice that shows the three-dimensional pattern of the entire crystal

cell volume.description: three-dimensional space occupied by one unit cell in a crystal lattice

cell formula units.description: number of formula units of a substance contained within one unit cell of its crystal lattice

density of crystal structure.description: mass of the atoms within a unit cell divided by the volume of the unit cell

strukturbericht designation.description: crystal structure classification system established by the Zeitschrift für Kristallographie

space group.description: space group name or number of the structure

crystal system.examples: cubic, hexagonal; trigonal; tetragonal; orthorhombic; monoclinic; triclinic;

Pearson symbol.examples: cP3; cF23; cl5; hR12; hP2; tl4; tP7; ol4; oF8; oC8; oP2; mC6; mP4; aP16

extinction symbol.description: symbol represents specific systematic absences in a crystal's diffraction pattern

perovskite type.examples: ABC3; inspired; 2d; 3d; Dion-Jacobson; Ruddlesden-Popper; quasi-2d; quasi-3d;

defect.description: defect of materials ranging from 0D point defects to 2D interface

0D defect.description: defects that occur only at or around a single lattice point

species.description: a specific chemcial species (element or molecule) constituting a point defect in the material

formation energy.description: defect formation energy, i.e. lattice cohesive energy in the case of vacancy

density.description: ratio of defected lattice sites to those containig atoms

electric charge.description: charge state of point defecs in the unit of electron

1D defect.description: linear crystallographic defect or irregularity within a crystal structure which contains an abrupt change in the arrangement of atoms

Burgers vector.description: magnitude and direction of the lattice distortion resulting from a dislocation

density.description: measure of the number of dislocations in a unit volume of a crystalline material

velocity.description: velocity of mobile dislocation upon applied stress

2D defect.description: planar defect separating two phases or crystal lattices, each of which may be solid, liquid, or gaseous

interfacial energy.description: energy required to form a unit area of new interface

misorientation.description: difference in crystallographic orientation between two crystallites in a polycrystalline material

misfit strain.description: the strain induced at the interface between two materials or phases due to a mismatch in their lattice parameters. &&&

3D defect.description: volumetric defect such as void, pores, or clusters in the materials, which is unintentionally incorporated in the materials.

volume fraction.description: volume fraction of the 3D defect with respect to the volume of whole materials

fraction.description: fraction of defect of the corresponding size

constituent.description: name of constituent such as element symbol, molecular fomula, molecule name, etc

concentration.description: concentration of the element in material

unit.description: unit of the composition value in at.% or wt.%

grain.description: inner structure of material made of individual crystalline areas

grain size.description: estimate of the average grain diameter

aspect ratio.description: length ratio between longest distance and shortest distance of grain &&&

Lotgering factor.description: A measurement to assess the crystallographic orientations of inorganic bulk materials. Typically calculated based on the intensities measured using X-ray diffraction pattern data. This factor serves as a convenient orientation index for determining the degree of crystallographic alignment in materials. Defined as (P-Po)/(1-Po) where P and Po are the intensity ratios for the sample and randomly oriented sample, respectively.

morphology.description: morphological data of materials of various shapes

2D nano.description: a substrance composed of single or few layers of atoms or molecules, exhibiting unique 2D properties

note.description: note describing essential information of 2D structure

3D bulk.description: Information of 3D bulk material; based on the shape, dimension can be described flexibly (in x-y-z or equivalent diameter); minimum dimension of each direction would be 10 mm.

x.description: length in x-direction of rectangular bulk material

y.description: length in y-direction of rectangular bulk material

z.description: length in z-direction of rectangular bulk material

diameter.description: equivalent diameter in spherical bulk; approximate value of the equivalent diameter of 3D bulk material of shperical but irregular shape.

bar.description: a solid object that has a long, narrow, and typically rectangular or square cross-section. In general, the dimension of each bae can be different. ***

base dimension.description: dimension of rectangular or square shape bases of bar

x.description: length of rectangular base in x-direction

y.description: length of rectangular base in y-direction

cylinder.description: a solid object that has a long, narrow, and circular bases at each end. In general, the diameter of each base can be different. ***

diameter.description: diameter of circular bases of each end

dimension.description: dimension of disk (two dimensional geometric object characterized by diameter of circular surface and thickness)

shape.examples: sphere; rod; plate; cube, rectangle; octahedron; truncated dodecahedron

diameter.description: average diameter of spherical or rod shaped particles

length.description: average length of rod shaped particles

x.description: dimension in x direction of plate shaped particles

y.description: dimension in y direction of plate shaped particles

z.description: dimension in z direction of rectangular shaped particles

height.description: average height or thickness of plate shaped particles

facet.description: facet orientation of the plate particle

plate.description: Information of plate or sheet; plate is distinguishable from sheet in their thickness. A sheet is thinner tha a plate. Generallay, thickness of a sheet is typically 0.1~6 mm, while that of a plate is usually larger than 6 mm. ***

dimension.description: dimension of the plate in the form of xyt

x.description: length of the plate in x-direction

y.description: length of the plate in y-direction

solution.description: single phase material where a polymer dissolves in a suitable solvent. Polymer chains are dispersed throughout the solvent, which can be liquid or gas ***

polymer.description: polymer chain dessolved in the solution

solvent.description: the medium in which the polymer dissolves.

uniformity.description: uniformity of the thickness in terms of the range of thickness variation

tube.description: Information of 1D tube materials including carbon nanotube

average diameter.description: average value of inner and outer diameters of the tube

orientation.description: orientation in the direction of length

aspect ratio.description: the ratio of width to its height &&&

phase.description: information of phases in a material

data.description: array data of phases in a material

id.decription: id of phase for internal use of the dataset, that would be be used for defining materials structure

name.description: name of a phase according to its thermodynamic state and/or crystal structure

constituent.description: name of constituent such as element symbol, molecular fomula, molecule name, etc

concentration.description: concentration of the element in material

unit.description: unit of the composition value in at.% or wt.%

fraction.description: relative amounts of phase_n in the microstructure
fraction.alias: phase amounts, volume fraction of phases

polymer blend type.examples: random; rubbery; continuous phase; dispersed phase; spherical domain; layered; fibrous; networked; tangled

pore diameter.description: size of the pore in material; in some cases, pores can have multiple number of distinguishable sizes

average pore diameter.description: mean diameter of pores (or voids) present in a given material

pore limiting diameter.description: diameter of the largest probe that can traverse through pore channels

pore fraction.description: the fraction of a material's volume that is empty

electrochemically active surface area.description: electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of material_n

constituent.description: name of constituent such as element symbol, molecular fomula, molecule name, etc

concentration.description: concentration of the element in material

crystallography.description: the arrangement of atoms at the outermost layers of a solid material. It describes the geometric configuration, including the positions of atoms relative to each other, the presence of defects (like vacancies or adatoms), and the overall symmetry of the surface.

Miller indices.description: direction of surface normal in Miller indices; Miller indices is the notation in crystallography to describe the orientation of crystal planes and directions in a crystal lattice &&&

unit cell.description: the smallest repeating unit of a crysl structure at the surface of a material

texture table.description: relative probability of finding the crystals comprising a polycrystalline aggregate in various orientations

texture component.description: specific orientation distribution as a measure for the strength and scatter of a crystallographic texture

fraction.description: fraction of a specific orientation distribution as a measure for the strength and scatter of a crystallographic texture

uncertainty.description: uncertanty in the fraction of texture component

pole figure.description: graphical representation of the orientation of texture components

inverse pole figure.description: A plot showing the distribution of sample directions in the reference frame of a crystal. &&&

ODF section plot.description: URI of graphical representation of the Orientation Distribution Function (ODF) &&&

property.description: attribute, quality, or chracteristic of materials

biomedical property.description: properties that characterize the interaction and performance of materials in biological and medical applications @@@

biocompatibility.description: properties that characterize the compatibility between materials and biological systems

cytotoxicity.description: the level of toxicity of the materials on the cells

inflammatory response.description: the important mechanism by which the body responds to injury and infection

hemocompatibility.description: the core performance indicators of materials applied in the blood environment

bioactivity.description: ability of materials to induce specific biological responses

osteointegration.description: ability of the materials to form a direct bond with bone tissue

surface reactivity.description: the ability of the material surface to interact with the cell

biodegradability.description: the ability of materials to be gradually broken down, absorbed or metabolised in an organism

degradation rate.description: rate of decomposition of materials in the biological environment

degradation product.description: whether substances produced by the decomposition of the material are harmful to living organisms

antibacterial property.description: the ability of materials to inhibit or kill microorganisms

minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC).description: minimum concentration to inhibit microbial growth

zone of inhibition.description: the area where antibacterial substances (such as antibiotics, antimicrobials) have an inhibitory effect on microbial growth

antibacterial efficiency.description: the ability of materials or antibacterial agent to inhibit the growth and multiplication of bacteria

cytocompatibility.description: the ability of the materials to support cell attachment, proliferation and differentiation

cell adhesion rate.description: the ability of cells to adhere to the surface of the materials

cell proliferation rate.description: the rate of cell growth on the surface of the materials

cell differentiation.description: whether the material supports cell differentiation to specific functional cells

immunocompatibility.description: ability to avoid adverse immune responses

immune cell activation.description: cells in the immune system undergo a series of physiological and functional changes after being stimulated by specific signals

complement activation.description: defense mechanisms that help the body recognize, attack, and eliminate pathogens

tissue compatibility.description: compatibility of the materials with specific tissues (e.g. bone, cartilage, skin)

tissue integration.description: degree of bonding of the materials to the surrounding tissue

tissue regeneration.description: the process by which tissues or organs recover the structure and function after injury or damage through cell proliferation and differentiation

osteogenic property.description: the ability of the materials to promote bone tissue formation and repair

mineralised nodule formation.description: assessment of mineralisation by alizarin red staining

corrosion property.description: properties related to deterioration of a material as it reacts with its envirionment via electrochemical and chemical reactions

anodic partial current.description: sum of all the currents corresponding to anodic reactions on the electrode

cathodic partial current.description: sum of all the currents corresponding to cathodic electochemical reactions on the electrode

corrosion current.description: anodic partial current due to metal oxidation

corrosion potential.description: electrode potential of a metal in a given corrosion system

corrosion rate.description: amount of corrosion loss per year in thckness

free corrosion current.description: corrosion current at the free corrosion potential

free corrosion potential.description: corrosion potential in the absence of net (external) electrical current flowing to or from the metal surface; The potential is measured with repsect to the potential of the reference electrode.

passivation current.description: corrosion current at the passivation potential

passivation potential.description: corrosion potential, at which the corrosion current has a peak value, and above which there is a range of potentials, where the metal is in a passive state; The potential is measured with repsect to the potential of the reference electrode.

pitting initiation potential.description: the lowest value of the corrosion potential at which pit initiation is possible on a passive surface in a given corrosive environment; The potential is measured with repsect to the potential of the reference electrode.

polarized potential.description: potential across the electrode/electrolyte interface that is the sum of the corrosion potential and the applied polarization

redox potential.description: potential of a reversible oxidation-reduction reaction in a given electrolyte recorded on a S.H.E. scale

re-activation potential.description: corrosion potential below which reactivation takes place; The potential is measured with repsect to the potential of the reference electrode.

standard electrode potential.description: reversible potential for an electrode process when all products and reactants are at unit activity, recorded on a S.H.E scale.

transpassivation potential.description: corrosion potential above which the metal is in the transpassive state; The potential is measured with repsect to the potential of the reference electrode.

electrical property.description: physical properties that a material exhibits upon the application of electric forces

band gap bowing parameter.description: A parameter that decribes nonlinear variation of band gap between two materials with different lattice constants

band gap energy.description: an energy range in a solid where no electronic states can exist (between valence band and conduction band)
band gap energy.alias: energy band gap; band gap energy

band gap graded.description: TRUE if the band gap varies as a function of the vertical position of the materials i.e. in the perovskite layer.

carrier type.description: the type of charge carrier in a material (e.g., electron or hole)

carrier concentration.description: number of charge carriers per volume

carrier diffusion length.description: an average distance that the excess carriers can cover before they recombine

carrier lifetime.description: an average time it takes for a minority carrier to recombine

conduction band minimum.description: the lowest range of vacant electronic states.
conduction band minimum.alias: CBM, LUMO, lowest unoccupied molecular orbital

constituent.description: name of constituent such as element symbol, molecular fomula, molecule name, etc

charge.description: concentration of the element in material

critical temperature.description: temperature above which certain materials lose their permanent polarization properties

current density.description: the amount of charge per unit time that flows through a unit area of a chosen cross section at an applied voltage &&&

value.description: value of current density at an applied voltage

defect energy level.description: the energy level of the states introduced within the band gap of a semiconductor or insulator due to the presence of defects

dielectric constant.description: relative premittivity compared to the vacuum in induced polarization stored in a material under electric field.

dielectric strength.description: the maximum electric field that the material can withstand under ideal conditions without undergoing electrical breakdown and becoming electrically conductive

electric susceptibility.description: a dimesionless proportionality constant that indicates the degree of polarization of a material

electrical conductivity.description: quantity how strongly it conducts electric current

electron affinity.description: the amount of energy released (or required) when an atom, molecule, or material gains an electron in the gas phase &&&

electron effective mass.description: a mass that a particle seems to have when responding to forces or interacting with other identical particles in a thermal distribution

electron mobility.description: a value that represents how quickly a single electron can move through a metal or semiconductor, when pulled by an electric field

electronic density of states.description: proportion of states that are to be occupied by the system at each energy, submitted in the form of file or array number data

DOS data.alias: density of states data, DOS data

density of state.description: density of the state corresponding to the energy

DOS file.description: URI of the numberal array file of the density of state

exciton binding energy.description: a binding energy of an exciton (an electrically neutral quasiparticle that exists in insulators or semiconductors)

Fermi level.description: the thermodynamic work required to add one electron to the body

Hall coefficient.description: the ratio of the induced electric field to the product of the current density and the applied magnetic field

Hall voltage.description: Voltage difference across an electrical conductor that is transverse to an electric current in the conductor and to an applied magnetic field perpendicular to the current

hole effective mass.description: a mass that a particle seems to have when responding to forces or interacting with other identical particles in a thermal distribution

hole mobility.description: a value that represents how quickly a single hole can move through a metal or semiconductor, when pulled by an electric field

impedance.description: total opposition to an alternating current, including both resistance and reactance &&&

ionic activation energy.description: the energy barriers for ion transport

ionic conductivity.description: quantity how strongly it conducts ionic charge

ionic mobility.description: The proportionality factor between an ion's drift velocity in an electric field in a specified medium

loss tangent.description: signal loss due to the dielectric materials' inherent dissipation of electromagnetic energy. It is dependent on the frequency of applied electric field.

permittivity.description: measure of the electric polarizability of a material

resistivity.description: quantity how strongly it resists electric current

spin-Hall angle.description: When a current is applied to a material with a very large spin-orbit interaction, the current does not go straight due to the strong spin-orbit interaction, and different spins diverge in the width direction on both sodes of the sample, resulting in spin accumumation and spin current. The ratio of the generated spin current to the flowed current.

spin-orbit splitting energy.description: energy level splitting energy produced by the spin–orbit interaction of a particle's spin with its motion inside a potential

surface resistivity.description: resistance to electrical flow on the surface ***

trap density.description: the density of electronic trap states within dielectric materials responsible for carrier trapping and de-trapping

temperature coefficient of resistance.description: a change in resistance per unit resistance per degree rise in temperature based upon the resistance of 0 degree celcius

valence band maximum.description: the highest range of electron energies in which electrons are normally present at absolute zero temperature
valence band maximum.alias: VBM, HOMO, highest occupied molecular orbital

electrochemical property.description: property either caused or accompanied by the passage of an electric current

oxidation potential.description: the oxidation potential (voltage vs. Li/Li+) of solid or liquid materials (eg. the  solvent, the conductive salt or solid electrolytes)

reduction potential.description: the reduction potential (voltage vs. Li/Li+) of solid or liquid materials (eg. the  solvent, the conductive salt or solid electrolytes)

ferroelectric property.description: property of certain materials to have a spontaneous polarization which can be reversed by the application of an external electric field

coercive electric field.description: the strength of the electric field at which the macroscopic polarization of the ferroelectric capacitor disappears, presented by values assigned to symbols E_{c}

remanent polarization.description: polarization that remains after removing the electric field applied to an object, presented by values assigned to symbols P_{r}

saturation polarization.description: Maximum polarization when an electric field is applied to an object, presented by values assigned to symbols P_{s}

gas transmission property.description: gas transmission property through the encapsulation materials
gas transmission property.alias: moisture transmission; air transmission; hydrogen permeability; gas permeation property &&&

gas.description: gas transmission rate through the material
gas.examples: oxygen; air; water vapor; hydrogen; nitrogen

transmission rate.description: the rate at which a gas or vapor passes through a material, influenced by diffusion and solubility characteristics; In general, permeability of a gas is defined as the volume of the gas that passes through a materials of a unit thickness per unit area and unit time, under a unit partial-pressure difference

magnetic property.description: physical properties that a material exhibits upon the application of magnetic forces

absorption shielding effectiveness.description: amount of the attenuated amplitude of electromagnetic waves in the material, compared to the amount of the transmitted electromagnetic waves into the material

coercivity.description: the magnetic field required to reuce the magnetic flux of a material to zero

Curie temperature.description: temperature above which certain materials lose their permanent magnetic properties

DMI energy density.description: Anti-symmetric exchange interaction that favors a canted spin configuration between neighboring spins
DMI energy density.alias: D, Dzyloshinskii-Moriya interaction energy density, DMI constant, antisymmetric exchange energy constant, Dzyaloshinskii–Moriya interaction constant

DMI field.description: An effective in-plane magnetic field acting on a domain wall which tends to align the domain wall magnetization to the Neel configuration with a specific chirality

domain wall propagation field.description: the minimum magnetic field required to move or propagate a domain wall within a ferromagnetic material

effective magnetic anisotropy energy.description: Net perpendicular magnetic anisotropy which is derived from the first-order uniaxial anisotropy constant(Ku) by eliminating shape anisotropy term (2πMs2)

interface state density.description: the number of states per unit area of the interface per unit energy in the semiconductor band gap

intrinsic coercivity.description: the magnetic field required to reduce the magnetization of a material to zero

magnetic anisotropy constant.description: A measure of the energy necessary to align the magnetization from an easy axis to the hard axis of the specimen. The easy and hard axis is determined by crystal structure, interface and the shape of the specimen.
magnetic anisotropy constant.alias: magnetic anisotropy energy; anisotropy energy constant

magnetic anisotropy energy.description: Energy required to rotate the magnetization from an easy axis to a hard axis

magnetic anisotropy field.description: Magnetic field required for complete magnetization in the direction in which the largest magnetic field is required for magnetization in a magnetic material having magentic anisotropy

magnetic field strength.alias: magnetic flux density, magnetic induction, magnetic intensity

magnetic induction.description: the magnitude of the internal field strength (B) within a substance that is subjected to an externally applied magnetic field (H)

magneto-optical Kerr angle.description: Degree of ferromagnetic ordering of materials along with applied magnetic field direction, represented by the rotation angle of refelcted light polarization when the light is refleted from the surface of a magnetized material

magnetization.description: In the presence of an externally applied magnetic field (H), the magnetic moments within a material tend to become aligned with the field and to reinforce it by virtue of their magnetic fields

magnetostriction.description: The strain induced by a change in magnetization

maximum BH product.description: The product of the magnetic flux density and magnetic field strength of a permanent magnet at any point of any demagnetization curve.

maximum permeability.description: The maximum value of absolute permeability

maximum susceptibility.description: The maximum value of absolute susceptibility

permeability.description: the degree of magnetization of a material in response to a magnetic field $$$!@#

Neel temperature.description: emperature at which an antiferromagnetic material transitions to a paramagnetic state, meaning that thermal energy overcomes the exchange interactions maintaining the antiferromagnetic ordering. &&&

recoil permeability.description: The permeability corresponding to the slope of the recoil line

reflection shielding effectiveness.description: amount of the reflected electromagnetic waves from the material

relative permeability.description: relative permeability is the ratio of the permeability of a specific medium to the permeability of vacuume $$$!@#

remanent coercivity.description: the magnetic field required to reduce the remanent magnetization of a material to zero, meaning that when the H field is finally returned to zero, then both B and M also fall to zero (the material reaches the origin in the hysteresis curve)

remanent flux density.description: The value of the magnetic flux density remaining in a magnetized body when, in the absence of a self-demagnetizing field, the applied magnetic field strength is brought to zero

remanent magnetic polarization.description: The value of the magnetic polarization remaining in a magnetized body when, in the absence of a self-demagnetizing field, the applied magnetic field strength is brought to zero

remanent magnetization.description: The value of the magnetization remaining in a magnetized body when, in the absence of a self-demagnetizing field, the applied magnetic field strength is brought to zero

saturation flux density.description: The maximum magnetic induction (Bs) that results when all the magnetic dipoles in a solid piece are mutually aligned with the external field (H). A state reached when an increase in applied external magnetic field H cannot increase the magnetization (M) of the material further.

saturation magnetic polarization.description: The maximum obtainable magnetic polarization for a given substance at a given temperature

saturation magnetization.description: The maximum obtainable magnetization for a given substance at a given temperature

spin Hall angle.description: the conversion efficiency of the charge currents to pure spin currents

spin-orbit torque efficiency.description: The conversion factor of the current density to the spin-orbit torque-induced effective magnetic field on a magnetization

squareness.description: A parameter indicating how close the shape of the hysteresis curve is to a square

total shielding effectivenss.description: amount of the shielded electromagnetic waves from the material

work function.description: the minimum thermodynamic work (i.e., energy) needed to remove an electron from a solid to a point in the vacuum immediately outside the solid surface

mechanical property.description: physical properties that a material exhibits upon the application of forces

compressive property.description: properties indicate how the material will react to forces being applied in compression

compressive fracture strain.description: maximum deformation or strain a material can undergo in compression when it fails or fractures completely

compressive fracture strength.description: maximum stress or force per unit area that a material can withstand under compression when it fails or fractures completely

compressive yield strength.description: yield strength of material during compressive test

ultimate compressive strength.description: maximum compressive strength of material during compressive test

creep property.description: materials response upon long-term exposure to high levels of stress that are still below the yield strength of the materials

instantaneous strain.description: immediate deformation of materials upon initial application of a stress

time to tertiary creep.description: time necessary to reach tertiary creep stage

minimum creep rate.description: slope of the portion of the creep vs. time diagram corresponding to secondary creep

rupture time.description: time necessary produce failure while materials is subjected to constant load at a constant temperature

creep compliance.description: a measure of the deformation of a material under a constant stress over time &&&

creep rupture strength.description: stress causing fracture in a creep test at a given time, in a specified constant environment

creep rupture strain.description: total strain after creep rupture test

reduction of area.description: proportional reduction of the cross-sectional area of a creep test piece at the plane of fracture measured after fracture

stress exponent.description: the value of a stress exponent, n, obtained via creep rate measurements over a range of applied stress

elastic property.description: mechanical property of a material to withstand the compression or the elongation with respect to its length

bulk modulus.description: measure of how resistant to compression that substance is

elastic recovery ratio.description: ability to recover elastically after the removal of the elastic load in nano indentation; it is defined as the ratio of elastic revocery displacement (the amount of deformation recovered after the load is removed) to the maximum indentation depth during loading **#

Poisson's ratio.description: ratio of transverse strain to corresponding axial strain on a material stressed along one axis

resilience modulus.description: measure of resilience modulus defined as the maximum energy that can be absorbed per unit volume without creating permanent distortion

Young's modulus.description: elastic properties of materials undergoing tension or compression in one direction

fatigue property.description: materials response when subjected to cyclic load and/or strain

fatigue type.description: type of fatigue properties depending on number of cycles, applied stress and/or strain level and environment

fatigue life.description: number of stress (strain) cyclcles required to cause failure

fatigue limit.description: the highest stress that materials can withstand for an infinite number of cycles without breaking

fatigue crack growth rate.description: rate of crack extension under fatigue loading, expressed in terms of crack extension per cycle, da/dN

stress intensity factor.description: magnitude of the mathematically ideal, crack-tip stress field for a particular mode in a homogeneous, linear-elastic body

stress intensity factor range.description: variation in the stress intensity factor in a cycle in fatigue

fatigue crack growth threshold.description: asymptotic value of stress intensity factor range at which crack growth rate approaches zero

flexural property.description: properties indicate how the material will react to forces being applied in bending $$$!@# 추가

flexural modulus.description: the slope of a stress-strain curve produced by a flexural test

flexural strength.description: the stress in a material just before it yields in a flexure test

fracture toughness.description: generic term for measures of resistance to extension of a crack

stress intensity factor.description: magnitude of the mathematically ideal, crack-tip stress field for a particular mode in a homogeneous, linear-elastic body

plane strain fracture toughness K.description: crack-extension resistance under conditions of crack-tip plane strain in mode I and predominantly linear-elastic conditions

plane strain fracture toughness J.description: crack-extension resistance under conditions of crack-tip plane strain in mode I and substantial plastic deformation

crack tip opening displacement.description: crack displacement resulting from the total deformation at variously defined locations near the original crack tip

hardness.description: measure of the resistance to localized plastic deformation induced by either mechanical indentation or abrasion

impact toughness.description: toughness of a material using the value of impact energy absorbed by the maerial during fracturing under impact

impact energy.description: work done to fracture a test specimen measured by Charpy impact test

upper shelf energy.description: high fracture energy associated with ductile behavior

lower shelf energy.description: low fracture energy associated with brittle behavior

ductile-to-brittle transition temperatur.description: temperature at which there is a pronounced decrease in a material’s ability to absorb force without fracturing

shear property.description: properties indicate how the material will react to forces being applied in shear $$$!@# 전단 물성 추가

shear modulus.description: the ratio of shear stress to the shear strain

shear strength.description: strength of a material against the type of yield when the material fails in shear

sheet metal formability.description: The ease with which a sheet metal can be shaped through plastic deformation

plastic strain ratio.description: ratio of true plastic width strain to the true plastic thickness strain in a tensile test

normal anisotropy.description: ratio of the logarithmic strain in the transverse direction (TD) to the logarithmic strain in the normal direction (ND)

planar anisotropy.description: variation of plastic strain ratio with direction in the plane of the sheet

limiting drawing ratio.description: ratio of the maximum blank diameter that can be drawn into a cup without failure, to the diameter of the punch

bendability.description: ability of a material to be bent around a specified radius without fracture

hole expansion ratio.description: ratio of the expaned hole diameter to the initial hole diameter

springback ratio.description: ratio of the radius of curvature after springback to the radius at the end of forming

tensile property.description: properties indicate how the material will react to forces being applied in tension $$$ 조정

yield strength.description: The stress value at which the plastic deformation of a material begins to occur

ultimate tensile strength.description: maximum stress that can be applied to the material before it breaks
ultimate tensile strength.alias: ultimate tensile stress; UTS; R$_m$; tensile strength

uniform elongation.description: elongation at maximum load and immediately preceding the onset of necking in a tensile test

total elongation.description: percentage by which the material can be stretched before it breaks

strain hardening exponent.description: constant used in calculations relating to stress-strain bahavior in work hardening

reduction of area.description: proportional reduction of the cross-sectional area of a tensile test piece at the plane of fracture measured after fracture

critical resolved shear stress.description: component of shear stress necessary to initiate slip in a grain

tensile toughness.description: The amount of energy a material can absorb before it fractures characterized from the area under the stress-strain curve

fracture strength.description: maximum stress or force per unit area that a material can withstand under tensile condition when it fails or fractures completely

absorbance.description: the quantity of light absorbed by an object, defined as the logarithm of the ratio of incident to transmitted or reflected radiation power through a material

absorption coefficient.description: a value representing how far into a material light of a particular wavelength can penetrate before it is absorbed

electroluminescence.description: light emission in response to the passage of an electric current or to a strong electric field

lifetime.description: emissive decays time from the excited state

quantum efficiency.description: number of photons emitted per injected electrons

peak position.description: a wavelength where the intensity of electroluminescence reaches its maximum value; If more than one peak positions, separate those by comma

haze.description: amount of light that is scattered away from the direct optical path when passing through a transparent material. It is typically measured as the percentage of light that deviates by more than 2.5 degrees from the incident beam. &&&

optical band gap.description: optical band gap obtained from the absorption spectrum

photoluminescence.description: light emission from any form of matter after the absorption of photons

photoluminescence type.description: type of emission; Luminescence is classified according to the magnitude of the delay time between absorption and reemission events. If reemission occurs for times much less than 1 s, the phenomenon is termed fluorescence. If reemission occurs for longer than 1 second, the phenomenon is termed phosphorescence.

lifetime.description: emissive decays time from the excited state

quantum efficiency.description: number of photons emitted per absorbed photons of excitation source

peak position.description: a wavelength where the intensity of photoluminescence reaches its maximum value; If more than one peak positions, separate those by comma

maximum peak wavelength.description: wavelength at which a substance absorbs the maximum amount of ultraviolet light

reflectance.description: ratio of the radiation flux reflected by a sample surface to the incident radiation flux

transmittance.description: ratio of light that is transmitted through a material to the total incident light

normal incidence transmittance.description: ratio of the radiation flux normally transmitted through a sample to the incident radiation flux

yellowness index.description: a measure of how much a material has changed color from clear or white towards yellow. It is calculated from spectrophotometric data using the CIE Tristimulus values. &&&

refractive index.description: ratio of the speed of the light in the vacuum to that in a material

piezoelectric property.description: property of certain materials which induces an electric field when mechanical stress is applied OR VICE VERSA

piezoelectric strain coefficient.description: strain induced when polarized when electric field applied, presented by values assigned to symbols d*_{ij}, i.e d*_{11}, d*_{22}, d*_{33} etc.

piezoelectric voltage coefficient.description: voltage induced when polarized when stress applied, presented by values assigned to symbols g_{ij}, i.e g_{11}, g_{22}, g_{33} etc.

electromechanical coupling factor.description: conversion efficiency between electrical and mechanical energy,k^2

mechanical quality factor.description: Variables that indicate how little loss is in piezoelectric deformation (It is the inverse of the mechanical loss tan ϕ)

rheological property.description: The material properties that govern the specific way in which materials deform or flowing response to applied forces or stresses

inherent viscosity.description: equal to ln{(relative viscosity)} devided by mass density

intrinsic viscosity.description: reduced viscosity in infinite dilute solution of the polymer or limit of the value of inherent viscosity

reduced viscosity.description: specific viscosity value adjusted to account for the effects of concentration; equal to specific viscosity devided by mass density

dynamic viscosity.description: resistance of a fluid (liquid or gas) to a change in shape, or movement of neighbouring portions relative to one another; in general the dynamic viscosity is dependent on the shear rate. *** &&&

variability.description: behavior of change in viscosity according to the shear rate or shear stress ***

variable modulus.description: responce of fluid (slurry or liquid) resistance to a change in shape, or movement of neighbouring portions relative to one another by shear rate &&&

loss tangent.description: the ratio of loss modululs to storage modulus, indicating the viscoelastic balance of a material &&&

stress relaxation time.description:
the time it takes for a material to relax its internal stresses when a constant strain is applied. It is a measure of how quickly the material can dissipate the applied stress over time.
 &&&

surface property.description: properties to characterize the surface of materials &&&

contact angle.description: the angle formed betseen a liquid droplet and a solide surface, indicating surface wettability &&&

RMS surface roughness.description: The root mean square value (RMS) of the surface roughness expressed in nm (mapping: Perovskite DB 5.4); the irregularities or deviations in the texture of a surface, typically at a micro or nanoscale level. It quantifies the variations in height of the surface features from the ideal smooth surface.”

G_{1c} (Mode1).description: the energy release rate for opening mode (a tensile stress normal to the plane of the crack)

G_{2c} (Mode2).description: the energy release rate for sliding mode (a shear stress acting parallel to the plane of the crack and perpendicular to the crack front)

impact resistance.description: The material's ability to absorb energy and resist damage under impact loading.

post-impact residual strength.description: The remaining strength of the material after it has sustained an impact.

lamina properties.description: The mechanical and physical properties of a single composite ply (lamina), including its longitudinal and transverse Young’s modulus, shear modulus, and Poisson’s ratios. A lamina is defined as the smallest, homogeneous, and isotropic or anisotropic material layer in a laminated composite structure. A lamina is usually composed of a matrix material and reinforcing fibers, and its properties can be anisotropic, meaning they differ depending on the direction of the applied stress.

engineering coefficients.description: The set of elastic constants used in engineering notation to describe a lamina’s stress–strain response under normal and shear loading.

E1.description: Young’s modulus for fiber longitudinal direction

E2.description: Young’s modulus for fiber transverse direction

tply.description: Nominal thickness of fiber reinforced plastic lamina

Xc.description: Compressive strength for fiber longitudinal direction

Yt.description: Tensile strength for fiber transverse direction

Yc.description: Compressive strength for fiber transverse direction

anisotropic tensor properties.description: The components of the fourth-order stiffness or compliance tensor that characterize the directional (anisotropic) elastic behavior of a composite lamina.

elastic stiffness tensor.description: Anisotropic elastic stiffness tensor Cᵢⱼ of the lamina; a mathematical representation that describes the relationship between stress and strain in materials, particularly in the context of anisotropic materials, such as composites.

compliance tensor.description: Anisotropic elastic stiffness tensor Sᵢⱼ of the lamina; a mathematical representation that relates the strain in a material to the applied stress. It is the inverse of the elastic stiffness tensor.

laminate properties.description: the overall effective mechanical properties of a multilayer composite (laminate), derived from its stacking sequence and individual lamina properties, including in-plane and bending stiffness.

stacking sequence.description: The specific arrangement and orientation of layers in a laminated composite material

extensional stiffness.description: The extensional stiffness, also known as tensile stiffness or axial stiffness, is a material property that describes its resistance to stretching or elongation under a load.

bending stretching coupling stiffness.description: The coupling stiffness of a laminate that links in-plane forces to bending deformations (and vice versa).

bending stiffness.description: The bending stiffness is the resistance of a member against bending deflection/deformation.

failure criteria.description: Criteria used for predicting the failure of composite materials under various stresses and strains. $$$ 삭제할 것

maximum stress criterion.description: Predicts failure based on maximum stress in each principal direction.

maximum strain criterion.description: Predicts failure based on maximum strain in each principal direction.

Tsai-Wu criterion.description: Non-linear model for predicting composite failure by considering the combined effect of stresses.

hashin criterion.description: Predicts failure by distinguishing between fiber and matrix failure modes.

architecture.description:
The cell architecture with respect to the direction of current flow and the order in which layers are deposited. The two most common are nip (also referred to as normal) and pin (also referred to as inverted) but there are also a few others, e.g. Back contacted 
• nip architecture means that the electrons are collected at the substrate side. The typical examples is when a TiO2 electron selective contact is deposited between the perovskite and the substrate (e.g. SLG | FTO | TiO2-c |Perovskite | …)   
• pin architecture means that it instead is the holes that are collected at the substrate side. The typical examples is when a PEDOT:PSS hole selective contact is deposited between the perovskite and the substrate (e.g. SLG | FTO | PEDOT:PSS |Perovskite | …)

additional layer front.description: functional layer below the substrate, or on the opposite side of the substrate from with respect to the device layers

material.description: Material of the additional layer front. The layer can be a stack of layers made of material_(n) defined in the materials section. Every layer in the stack should be separated by a space, a vertical bar, and a space, i.e. (' | ').

thickness.description:
Thickness of the layers in the additional layer front. 
• Thickness of each layer in the stack should be separated by a space, a vertical bar, and a space, i.e. (' | '). 
• The layers must line up with the previous material field.
• Every layer in the stack have a thickness. If it is unknown, state this as ‘nan’
• If there are uncertainties, state the best estimate, e.g write 100 and not 90-110

function.description:
The function of the additional layers front
• The function must line up with the previous material field.
• Every layer should be separated by a space, a vertical bar, and a space, i.e. (‘ | ‘)
• If a layer has more than one function, e.g. A and B, list the functions in order and separate them with semicolons, as in (A; B)

substrate.description: configurational information of the substrate. With the substrate, we refer to any layer below the electron transport layer in a nip-device, and any laye rbelow the hole transport layer in a pin-device. The substrate can be a stack of layer of material_(n)

material.description: Material of the substrate. The substrate can be a stack of layers made of material_(n) defined in the materials section. Every layer in the stack should be separated by a space, a vertical bar, and a space, i.e. (' | ').

thickness.description:
Thickness of the layers of the substrate. 
• Thickness of each layer in the stack should be separated by a space, a vertical bar, and a space, i.e. (' | '). 
• The layers must line up with the previous material field.
• Every layer in the stack have a thickness. If it is unknown, state this as ‘nan’
• If there are uncertainties, state the best estimate, e.g write 100 and not 90-110

area.description: The total area in cm2 of the substrate over which the perovskite is deposited. This may be significantly larger than the cell area

material.description: Material of the frontcontact. The frontcontact can be a stack of layers made of material_(n) defined in the materials section. Every layer in the stack should be separated by a space, a vertical bar, and a space, i.e. (' | ').

n-type layer.description: configurational information of the n-type or electron transport layer. The layer can be a stack of material_(n). With the ETL, we refer to any layer between the substrate and the intrinsic layer in a nip-device, and any layer between the intrinsic layer and the back contact in a pin-device.
n-type layer.alias: electron transport layer; ETL; ETM; n-type materials

material.description: Material of the n-type layer. The layer can be a stack of layers made of material_(n) defined in the materials section. Every layer in the stack should be separated by a space, a vertical bar, and a space, i.e. (' | ').

thickness.description:
Thickness of the layers of the electron transport layer. 
• Thickness of each layer in the stack should be separated by a space, a vertical bar, and a space, i.e. (' | '). 
• The layers must line up with the previous material field.
• Every layer in the stack have a thickness. If it is unknown, state this as ‘nan’
• If there are uncertainties, state the best estimate, e.g write 100 and not 90-110

intrinsic layer.description:
information of the photo-absorber layer. Perovskite is used here. 
The layer can be a stack of layers made of material_(n) defined in the materials section. Every layer in the stack is sequentially listed one by one using the keywords "intrinsic layer_(n)".

material.description: Material of the intrinsic layer.

thickness.description:
Thickness of the layers of the intrinsic layer
• Every layer in the stack have a thickness. If it is unknown, state this as ‘nan’
• If there are uncertainties, state the best estimate, e.g write 100 and not 90-110

material.description: Material of the p-type layer. The layer can be a stack of material_(n) defined in the materials section. Every layer in the stack should be separated by a space, a vertical bar, and a space, i.e. (' | ').
material.alias: hole transport layer; HTL; HTM; p-type materials

thickness.description:
A list of thicknesses of the individual layers in the stack.
• Every layer should be separated by a space, a vertical bar, and a space, i.e. (‘ | ‘)

• Every layer in the stack have a thickness. If it is unknown, state this as ‘nan’

total area.description: The total cell area in cm2. The total area is defined as the area that would provide photovoltaic performance when illuminated without any shading, i.e. in practice the geometric overlap between the top and bottom contact.

area measured.description: The effective area of the cell during IV and stability measurements under illumination. If measured with a mask, this corresponds to the area of the hole in the mask. Otherwise this area is the same as the total cell area. (mapping: Perovskite DB 5.4)

designated illumination area.description: light illuminated area of the module in cm^{2}, including both active and interconnection areas

JV data recalculated per cell.description: The preferred way to report IV data for modules is to recalculate the IV data to average data per sub-cells in the module. That simplifies downstream comparisons, and it ensures that there is no erroneous transformation that otherwise may occur when error checking the IV data. Mark this as TRUE if the conversation is done.

color rendering index.description: a measurement of how natual colors render under an artificial white light source when compared with sunlight. The index is measured from 0-100, with a perfect 100 indicating that colors of objects under the light source appear the same as they would under natural sunlight.

wavelength range.description: the wavelength range under which the average visible transmittance is determined (mapping: Perovskite DB 5.4)

stability.description:
stability data of photovoltaic device
• There is no sharp boundary between a stability measurement and a measurement of stabilised efficiency. Generally, a measurement under a few minutes is considered as a measurement of stabilised efficiency, whereas a stability measurement is sufficiently long for degradation to be seen (unless the device is really good)

initial value.description:
The efficiency, PCE, of the cell before the stability measurement routine starts
• Give the efficiency in %
• If there are uncertainties, only state the best estimate, e.g. write 20.5 and not 19-20
• If unknown or not applicable, leave this field empty.

burn in observed.description:
TRUE if the performance has a relatively fast initial decay after which the decay rate stabilises at a lower level.
• There are no sharp boundary between an initial burn in phase an a catastrophic failure, but if the performance of the cell quickly degrade by more than half, it is stretching it a bit to label this as an initial burn in phase.

final value.description:
The efficiency, PCE, of the cell at the end of the stability measurement routine 
• Give the efficiency in % (of the initial value of PCE)
• If there are uncertainties, only state the best estimate, e.g. write 20.5 and not 19-20
• If unknown or not applicable, leave this field empty.

T95.description:
The time after which the cell performance has degraded by 5 % with respect to the initial performance.
• If there are uncertainties, only state the best estimate, e.g. write 1000 and not 950-1050
• If unknown or not applicable, leave this field empty.

Ts95.description:
The time after which the cell performance has degraded by 5 % with respect to the performance after any initial burn in phase.
• If there are uncertainties, only state the best estimate, e.g. write 1000 and not 950-1050
• If unknown or not applicable, leave this field empty.

T80.description:
The time after which the cell performance has degraded by 20 % with respect to the initial performance.
• If there are uncertainties, only state the best estimate, e.g. write 1000 and not 950-1050
• If unknown or not applicable, leave this field empty.

Ts80.description:
The time after which the cell performance has degraded by 20 % with respect to the performance after any initial burn in phase.
• If there are uncertainties, only state the best estimate, e.g. write 1000 and not 950-1050
• If unknown or not applicable, leave this field empty.

Te80.description:
An estimated T80 for cells that were not measured sufficiently long for them to degrade by 20 %. with respect to the initial performance.
• This value will by definition have a significant uncertainty to it, as it is not measured but extrapolated under the assumption linearity but without a detailed and stabilised extrapolation protocol. This estimate is, however, not without value as it enables a rough comparison between all cells for with the stability has been measured. 
• If there is an experimental T80, leave this field empty.

Tse80.description:
An estimated Ts80 for cells that was not measured sufficiently long for them to degrade by 20 %. with respect to the performance after any initial burn in phase.
• This value will by definition have a significant uncertainty to it, as it is not measured but extrapolated under the assumption linearity but without a detailed and stabilised extrapolation protocol. This estimate is, however, not without value as it enables a ruff comparison between all cells for with the stability has been measured. 
• If there is an experimental T80s, leave this field empty.

• If there are uncertainties, only state the best estimate, e.g. write 20.5 and not 19-20
• If unknown or not applicable, leave this field empty.
