number of cells averaged.description:
The number of cells the reported IV data is based on.
• The preferred way to enter data is to give every individual cell its own entry in the data template/data base. If that is done, the data is an average over 1 cell.
• If the reported IV data is not the data from one individual cell, but an average over N cells. Give the number of cells.
• If the reported value is an average, but it is unknown over how many cells the value has been averaged (and no good estimate is available), state the number of cells as 2, which is the smallest number of cells that qualifies for an averaging procedure.

certified.description: TRUE if the IV data is measured by an independent and certification institute. If your solar simulator is calibrated by a calibrated reference diode, that does not count as a certified result.

certification institute.description: The name of the certification institute that has measured the certified device.

storage before measurement.description: data for storage of the sample before measurement

age of cell.description: The age of the cell with respect to the last deposition step was finalized

atmosphere.description:
The atmosphere in which the sample was stored between the device finaliastion and the IV measurement.
• If the atmosphere is a mixture of different gases, e.g. A and B, list the gases in alphabetic order and separate them with semicolons, as in (A; B)
• “Dry air” represent air with low relative humidity but where the relative humidity is not known
• “Ambient” represent air where the relative humidity is not known. For ambient conditions where the relative humidity is known, state this as “Air”
• “Vacuum” (of unspecified pressure) is for this purpose considered as an atmospheric gas
• If the atmosphere has changed during the storing time, separate the different atmospheres by a double forward angel bracket with one blank space on both sides (‘ >> ‘)

relative humidity.description:
the relative humidity in the atmosphere in which the sample was stored between the device finaliastion and the IV measurement.
• Relative humidity is the actual amount of water vapor in the air compared to the total amount of vapor that can exist in the air at its current temperature
• If the relative humidity has changed during the storing time, separate the different relative humidity by a double forward angel bracket with one blank space on both sides (‘ >> ‘)
• If the relative humidity is not known, stat that as ‘nan’
• For values with uncertainties, state the best estimate, e.g. write 35 and not 30-40.

atmosphere.description:
The atmosphere in which the IV measurement is conducted
• If the atmosphere is a mixture of different gases, e.g. A and B, list the gases in alphabetic order and separate them with semicolons, as in (A; B)
• “Dry air” represent air with low relative humidity but where the relative humidity is not known
• “Ambient” represent air where the relative humidity is not known. For ambient conditions where the relative humidity is known, state this as “Air”
• “Vacuum” (of unspecified pressure) is for this purpose considered as an atmospheric gas

relative humidity.description:
the relative humidity in which the IV measurement is conducted
• Relative humidity is the actual amount of water vapor in the air compared to the total amount of vapor that can exist in the air at its current temperature 
• If there are uncertainties, only state the best estimate, e.g write 35 and not 20-50.
• If the relative humidity is not known, stat that as ‘nan’

temperature.description:
The temperature of the device during the IV-measurement 
• If the temperature is not controlled and not is known, assume a standard room temperature of 25°C.
• If there are uncertainties, only state the best estimate, e.g write 35 and not 20-50.

light source.description: data on light source used for JV measurement

source type.description:
The type of light source used during the IV-measurement
• This category was included after the projects initial phase wherefor the list of reported categories is 
short. Thus, be prepared to expand the given list of alternatives in the data template. 
• The category Solar simulator should only be used when you do not really know which type of light source you have in your solar simulator.
source type.examples: laser; metal halide; outoor; solar simulator; sulfur plasma; white LED; Xenon plasma

instrument.description:
The brand name and model number of the light source/solar simulator used.
• This category was included after the projects initial phase where for the list of reported categories is 
short. Thus, be prepared to expand the given list of alternatives in the data template.
instrument.examples: Newport model 91192; Newport AAA; Atlas suntest

simulator classification.description:
Classification of the light source/solar simulator used during the IV measurement according to the IEC 60904-9 Ed.3
• A three-letter code consisting of A+, A, B, and C. The order of the letters represents the quality of: spectral match, spatial non-uniformity, and temporal instability of the light source/solar simulator.

light.description: data of the characteristics of light illuminated during JV measurement

intensity.description:
The radiant power per unit area of light incident on a surface. In the case of the IV measurement of a photovoltaic device, it means the irradiance of the light source/solar simulator used during the measurement.
• If there are uncertainties, only state the best estimate, e.g. write 100 and not 90-100.
• Standard AM 1.5 illumination correspond to 100 mW/cm2
• If you need to convert from illumination given in lux; at 550 nm, 1 mW/cm2 corresponds to 6830 lux. Be aware that the conversion change with the spectrum used. As a rule of thumb for general fluorescent/LED light sources, around 0.31mW corresponded to 1000 lux. If your light intensity is measured in lux, it probably means that your light spectra deviates quite a lot from AM 1.5, wherefore it is very important that you also specify the light spectra in the next column.

type of spectrum.description: The spectral type of the light source/solar simulator used during the IV measurement
type of spectrum.examples: AM 1.0; AM 1.5D; AM 1.5G; indoor light; monochromatic; natural sunlight; UV

wavelength range.description:
The wavelength range of the light source
• Separate the lower and upper bound by a comma
• For monochromatic light sources, give the same value for min and max values.
• If there are uncertainties, only state the best estimate, e.g. write 100 and not 90-100.
• State unknown values as ‘nan’

illumination direction.description:
The direction of the illumination with respect to the device stack
• If the cell is illuminated through the substrate, state this as ‘Substrate’
• If the cell is illuminated through the top contact, state this as ‘Superstrate’
• For back contacted cells illuminated from the non-contacted side, state this as ‘Superstrate’

masked cell.description: TRUE if the cell is illuminated through a mask with an opening that is smaller than the total cell area.

mask area.description:
The area of the opening in the mask through with the cell is illuminated (if there is a mask)
• If there are uncertainties, only state the best estimate, e.g. write 100 and not 90-100.
• If there is no light mask, leave this field empty.

scan.description: data of parameter scan condition during IV measurement

sweep rate.description: The speed of the potential sweep during the IV measurement

delay time.description:
The time at each potential value before integration in the potential sweep.
• For some potentiostats you need to specify this value, whereas for others it is set automatically and is not directly accessible.
• If there are uncertainties, only state the best estimate, e.g. write 100 and not 90-100.
• If unknown, leave this field empty.

integration time.description:
The integration time at each potential value in the potential sweep.
• For some potentiostats you need to specify this value, whereas for others it is set automatically and is not directly accessible.
• If there are uncertainties, only state the best estimate, e.g. write 100 and not 90-100.
• If unknown, leave this field empty.

voltage step.description:
The distance between the measurement point in the potential sweep
• If unknown, leave this field empty.

voltage settling time.description: the time takes for the voltage across the solar cell to reach a stable and constant value after a change in the external circuit conditions

preconditioning.description: preconditioning condition if done before IV measureent

protocol.description:
Any preconditioning protocol done immediately before the IV measurement
• If no preconditioning was done, state this as ‘none’
• If more than one preconditioning protocol was conducted in parallel, separate them with semicolons
• If more than one preconditioning protocol was conducted in sequence, separate them by a double forward angel bracket (‘ >> ‘)
protocol.examples: cooling; heating; light soacking; light soaking >> potential biasing; potential biasing

time.description:
The duration of the preconditioning protocol
• If there are uncertainties, only state the best estimate, e.g. write 100 and not 90-100.
• If unknown or not applicable, leave this field empty.

potential.description:
The potential at any potential biasing step
• If there are uncertainties, only state the best estimate, e.g. write 100 and not 90-100.
• If unknown or not applicable, leave this field empty.

light intensity.description:
The light intensity at any light soaking step
• If there are uncertainties, only state the best estimate, e.g. write 100 and not 90-100.
• If unknown or not applicable, leave this field empty.

electrostatic interaction.description: long range solver for electrostatic interaction; mathematical adjustment of interaction potential between two particles separated by long distances longer than a specified cutoff distance; empirical potential methods often use the cutoff distance to make the calculations efficient limiting the number of pairs for which the interactions should be calculated. In this case, the interactions of particles far more apart than the cutoff distances is ignored. However, in many cases, especially with electrostatic interactions these long-range interactions still have significant contribution to the true potential energy. Long-range potential corrections are applied to include the effect of long range interactions.

number of cells averaged.description:
The number of cells the reported stability data is based on.
• The preferred way to enter data is to give every individual cell its own entry in the data template/data base. If that is done, the data is an average over 1 cell.
• If the reported stability data is not the data from one individual cell, but an average over N cells. Give the number of cells.
• If the reported value is an average, but it is unknown over how many cells the value has been averaged (and no good estimate is available), state the number of cells as 2, which is the smallest number of cells that qualifies for an averaging procedure.

load condition.description:
The load situation of the illumination during the stability measurement.
• If the illumination is constant during the entire stability measurement, or if the cell is stored in the dark, state this as ‘Constant’. 
• If the situation periodically is interrupted by IV-measurements, continue to consider the load condition as constant
• If there is a cycling between dark and light, state this as ‘Cycled’
• If the illumination varies in an uncontrolled way, state this as ‘Uncontrolled’
• This category was included after the projects initial phase wherefor the list of reported categories is 
short. Thus, be prepared to expand the given list of alternatives in the data template.

cyclng times.description:
If the illumination load is cycled during the stability measurement, state the time in low light followed by the time in high light for the cycling period. 
• If not applicable, leave blank

potential bias.description: potential bias applied during stability measurement

load condition.description:
The Potentiostatic load condition during the stability measurement
• When the cell is not connected to anything, state this as ‘Open circuit’

range.description:
The potential range during the stability measurement
• The lower and upper bound recorded in number list format of Python

• If there are uncertainties, only state the best estimate, e.g. write 1 and not 0.90-1.1
• State unknown values as ‘nan’

This information can be inferred from data entered on the concentration for all reaction solutions but this gives a convenient shorthand filtering option. 
• If more than one component is in excess, order them in alphabetic order and separate them by semicolons.
• It there are no components that are in excess, write Stoichiometric

state.description:
The physical state of the preursor
• The three basic categories are Solid/Liquid/Gas
• Most cases are clear cut, e.g. spin-coating involves species in solution and evaporation involves species in gas phase. For less clear-cut cases, consider where the reaction really is happening as in:
o For a spray-coating procedure, it is droplets of liquid that enters the substrate (thus a liquid phase reaction) 
o For sputtering and thermal evaporation, it is species in gas phase that reaches the substrate (thus a gas phase reaction)
• This category was included after the projects initial phase wherefor the list of reported categories is short. Thus, be prepared to expand the given list of alternatives in the data template.

quenching.description:
Rapid cooling of the specimen from high temeprature for manipulating microstructure or crystallinity of materials.

In perovskite synthesis process, quencing is a measure to discontinuosly acceleragte the crystallization 
• The most common case is the antisolvent treatment where a volume of a solvent in which the perovskite is not soluble is poured on the substrate during spin coating.
• The same effect can also be achieved by blowing a gas on the sample
• If the sample quickly after spin coating is subjected to a vacuum, this also counts as quenched induced crystallisation

• If the sample quickly after spin coating was subjected to a vacuum, state this as ‘Vacuum’
• If an antisolvent was used but it is unknown which one, stat this as “Antisolvent”
• If no antisolvent was used, leave this field blank

publication.description: information about the source publication from which the data was obtained, including bibliographic identity (DOI, year, journal), research scope, samples studied, extracted figures/tables/passages. Bibliographic information beyond DOI is populated typically only in the enriched JSON variant (sourced from Scopus CSV or Crossref) since such information is not verbatim from the paper text ^-^

DOI.description: Digital Object Identifier of the publication; with this single identifier, all bibliographic fields can be auto-completed via Crossref (www.crossref.org), in line with the existing behavior of metadata.data source ^-^

Starrydata paper id.description: Starrydata paper id (SID, integer); optional, populated when the paper is registered in Starrydata ^-^

title.description: title of the publication. Typically populated only in the enriched JSON variant since it is sourced from external bibliographic data (Scopus CSV) rather than being verbatim from the paper text ^-^
title.examples: Effect of Ball-Milling Conditions on Thermoelectric Properties of Polycrystalline CuGaTe2

authors.description: list of authors of the publication. Typically populated only in the enriched JSON variant ^-^

journal.description: journal of the publication. Typically populated only in the enriched JSON variant ^-^

year.description: year of publication, integer. The most commonly used 'publication year' for citations. For more detailed date information including month, day, and event types (received, accepted, published online, published, corrected), see the 'dates' field ^-^

dates.description: detailed dates of various publication events. Each value is an ISO 8601 date string (YYYY-MM-DD), or partial form (YYYY, YYYY-MM), or null if unknown. Common events: received, accepted, published online, published, corrected. Additional event types can be added as needed (e.g. submitted, revised) ^-^

received.description: date the manuscript was received by editor ^-^

accepted.description: date the manuscript was accepted for publication ^-^

published.description: date of formal print/issue publication (when the article was assigned to a specific volume/issue) ^-^

corrected.description: date of correction or erratum, if any ^-^

article number.description: article number for journals using article-number-based pagination (e.g. Nature Communications, Scientific Reports). When article number is not used but a page range is desired in this field, use single-hyphen page range (e.g. '1215-1218'). Null when neither applies ^-^

abstract.description: abstract of the publication. Typically populated only in the enriched JSON variant since the abstract is sourced from external bibliographic data (Scopus CSV) and may not be verbatim from the paper text ^-^

author keywords.description: list of keywords assigned by the authors. Typically populated only in the enriched JSON variant ^-^

references.description: list of DOIs of the works referenced by this publication. Useful for citation network analysis. Typically populated only in the enriched JSON variant ^-^

citation.description: structured citation block providing constituent parts and ready-to-use formatted citation strings. Typically populated only in the enriched JSON variant ^-^

open access status.description: whether the publication is openly accessible; can be auto-determined via Unpaywall (api.unpaywall.org) using the DOI. Important for downstream automated extraction since paywalled PDFs cannot be redistributed ^-^

OA URL.description: URL of the open-access version of the publication (preprint, accepted manuscript, or publisher OA version) if one is available; can be auto-populated via Unpaywall (api.unpaywall.org) using the DOI ^-^

samples.description: samples studied in the publication; serves as the namespace of sample local ids referenced by figures, graphs, tables, and passages via 'samples' arrays ^-^

text passages.description: passages of body text from the publication that serve as data sources, peer to figures[] and tables[] ^-^

comments.description: free-form notes about the publication; useful for capturing context such as related works, errata, retraction status, or any narrative information that does not fit into the structured fields ^-^
comments.examples: see also follow-up paper at DOI 10.xxxx/yyyy; corrigendum issued 2024-06

scope.description: characterization of the publication's research scope; contains both content flags (experimental / theoretical) and an extensible list of classification codes from any classification scheme ^-^

paradigms.description: research paradigms employed in this publication, based on Jim Gray's Four Paradigms of Science (Hey, Tansley, Tolle 2009). Multiple paradigms can be selected for publications spanning more than one approach (e.g. experiment + simulation, theory + data-driven) ^-^

purposes.description: list of objectives or goals of the publication. Multiple purposes can coexist (e.g., proposing a new material AND investigating its underlying mechanism). Each entry is a free-text statement ^-^
purposes.examples: ["Improve thermoelectric ZT of CuGaTe2 by ball-milling treatment", "Investigate the effect of milling time on grain size and carrier mobility"]

approaches.description: list of methods or techniques used to achieve the purposes. Multiple approaches typically coexist in a single paper (synthesis + measurement + analysis). Each entry is a free-text statement ^-^
approaches.examples: ["Spark plasma sintering of ball-milled powder mixtures", "Hall measurement (van der Pauw geometry) for carrier characterization", "DFT calculations using VASP for band structure analysis"]

conclusions.description: logically rigorous conclusions of the publication, each presented with the preconditions under which it holds. Each conclusion is the LLM's strict reformulation of the authors' claims, stripped of rhetoric and emphasizing the conditions (synthesis parameters, measurement temperature, sample composition, etc.) required for the result to be valid. This enables reliable comparison across papers and machine-learning use. Multiple independent conclusions are allowed; each is a separate array element ^-^

text.description: the conclusion statement, written in natural language with explicit preconditions. Avoid authors' rhetorical language ('excellent', 'outstanding'); state the factual claim with all qualifying conditions ^-^
text.examples: ZT=1.4 was achieved by Bi2Te3 + 2wt% graphene composite at 500 K, sintered by SPS at 723 K, 50 MPa, 5 min, ball-milled 12 h at 250 rpm under Ar atmosphere

samples.description: list of sample local ids that this conclusion concerns ^-^

classifications.description: research field classification codes from one or more classification schemes; each entry pairs a scheme name with a code so that arbitrary current or future schemes can be recorded ^-^

comments.description: free-form notes about the scope characterization, such as the rationale for choosing particular classification schemes or any nuances in the classification ^-^
comments.examples: primarily a thermoelectric paper but with significant magnetic property discussion; chose Korea MSIT plus arXiv to capture both engineering and physics framings

classification.description: a single classification code under one classification scheme; the scheme field is intentionally an open string so that any classification system (e.g. Korea MSIT, arXiv, Scopus ASJC, MeSH, MSC, ACM CCS, Web of Science, or any future scheme) can be recorded without modifying the schema ^-^

name.description: human-readable name of the classification category, useful so that the meaning of the code is immediately apparent without resolving the reference ^-^
name.examples: Nano materials; Condensed matter - materials science

scheme.description: name of the classification scheme; free-form string to allow any current or future classification system ^-^
scheme.examples: Korea MSIT; arXiv; Scopus ASJC; MeSH; ACM CCS

code.description: classification code as defined by the scheme ^-^

sample.description: a sample or specimen studied in the publication; this is a paper-specific entity that becomes apparent only after reading the paper, conceptually closer to a system (processed material with specific properties) than to a single material. Samples are represented as a list of components combined according to a 'mixing type' (pure substance, solid solution, composite, etc.), naturally accommodating diverse cases from pure substances to multi-phase systems. Each component links to known compounds in external databases such as Materials Project or ICSD ^-^

sample local id.description: local identifier for this sample within the JSON document, used as the reference target from figures, graphs, tables, and passages via 'samples shown'. Format: 's-NNN' where NNN is a zero-padded 3-digit serial number (s-001, s-002, ...). This identifier is internal to the extracted JSON and does not depend on Starrydata or any other external system ^-^

Starrydata sample id.description: Starrydata sample id (integer); optional, populated only after the sample is registered or matched in Starrydata. When present, it supplements the sample local id for cross-system identification ^-^

name.description: name of the sample as written in the publication; may include special characters such as subscripts, Greek letters, slashes, or spaces. This field is for human readability and consistency verification (e.g. against Starrydata sample_name); it is not used for machine identification ^-^

description.description: free-form description of this sample, providing context that does not fit into the structured fields. Useful for capturing nuances such as synthesis route variations, post-processing treatments, special storage conditions, or any narrative information from the publication that gives the sample its identity ^-^
description.examples: spark plasma sintered at 723 K under 50 MPa for 5 minutes; annealed at 573 K for 24 h after sintering; hot-pressed pellet with [001] preferred orientation

mixing type.description: describes how the components are combined in this sample. Common values include 'pure substance' (single component), 'solid solution' (chemically unified single phase, e.g. Bi2Te2.7Se0.3 expressed as Bi2Te3 + Bi2Se3 with mole fractions), 'composite' (mechanically combined separate phases, e.g. Bi2Te3 matrix with graphene filler), 'stoichiometric compound', 'core-shell', 'matrix-precipitate', or other field-specific descriptions. Free-form string to accommodate the wide variety of mixing modes encountered in materials research ^-^
mixing type.examples: pure substance; solid solution; composite; stoichiometric compound; core-shell; matrix-precipitate

components.description: components that make up this sample; for pure substances, a single-element list; for composites, multiple components with their roles, fractions, and references to known compounds in external databases ^-^

component.description: a single component of a sample studied in the publication; samples are represented as a list of components, where a pure substance is the special case of a single-component sample and composites or solid solutions have multiple components. Each component has a required name (chemical formula or common name) and optional references[] to external databases (Materials Project, ICSD, COD, etc.) that may list the same compound. Components without external database entries (in-house compounds, intermediates, or unidentified phases) can be recorded with name only. Includes a free-form role label, structured fraction (value + unit) for machine processing, and a free-form fraction description for nuance ^-^

name.description: name of the referenced compound (e.g. chemical formula or common name) as recorded in the external database; convenient for human readability so that the meaning of the id is immediately apparent without resolving the reference ^-^

references.description: list of references that identify this component in external databases; a component can be referenced by multiple databases (e.g. the same compound has different IDs in Materials Project, ICSD, and COD). Optional: components without external database entries (in-house compounds, intermediates, unidentified phases) may have an empty list or omit this field ^-^

description.description: free-form description of this component, including details such as purity, supplier, lot number, particle size before mixing, pre-processing, or any other component-specific information that does not fit into the structured fields ^-^
description.examples: 99.999% purity from Sigma-Aldrich; ball-milled to 50 nm before mixing; synthesized in-house via hydrothermal method

role.description: free-form description of the component's role in the sample; the role vocabulary varies widely across fields (e.g. matrix/filler/dopant in composites, electrode/electrolyte/separator in batteries, support/active phase in catalysts), so this is left as an open string ^-^
role.examples: matrix; filler; dopant; support; n-type layer; active material

fraction value.description: numerical value of the component fraction ^-^

fraction unit.description: unit of the component fraction; common values include mol%, wt%, vol%, atomic%, mass fraction, mole fraction ^-^

fraction description.description: free-form description of the fraction, useful for qualitative or imprecise reports such as 'trace amount', 'approximately 5 wt%', or batch-to-batch variation notes ^-^
fraction description.examples: trace amount; approximately 5 wt%; slight variation between batches

reference.description: a single reference linking the parent component to an entry in an external database. The scheme field is intentionally an open string so that any database (Materials Project, ICSD, COD, AFLOW, KMDS-internal materials, or any future database) can be referenced without modifying the schema. A single component may have multiple references, recording the same compound across multiple databases ^-^

name.description: human-readable name of the referenced compound; useful when it differs from the parent component's name (for example, when a database entry uses a different formula convention) ^-^

scheme.description: name of the external database or reference scheme; free-form string ^-^
scheme.examples: Materials Project; ICSD; COD; AFLOW; KMDS material

id.description: identifier of the entry in the referenced database ^-^

figure.description: a figure extracted from the source publication ^-^

figure local id.description: local identifier for this figure within the JSON document. Format: 'f-NNN' ^-^

Starrydata figure id.description: Starrydata figure id (integer); optional, populated after registration ^-^

figure name.description: name of the figure as printed in the publication. Used for human readability and consistency verification; not for identification ^-^

caption summary.description: short summary of the figure caption ^-^

structure.description: brief structural description of the figure; a short summary of the visual structure (panels, axes, types, data series count). Suitable for one-glance overview ^-^
structure.examples: two-panel scatter plot with shared x-axis; single panel line graph; table with 4 columns and 12 rows

description.description: detailed objective textual description of the figure, sufficient for accessibility (visually impaired users) and for readers to reconstruct the underlying data without seeing the original image. Should include scale bars, axis structures, axis ticks, color coding, marker types, line styles, areas of overlap, axis discontinuities (broken axes), legend position, and any other visual details that affect interpretation. This field describes only what is objectively present, not how to extract data from it ^-^
description.examples: Plot has dual y-axes. Left y-axis (linear, 0-200 mu V/K) for Seebeck coefficient (red and blue curves). Right y-axis (log scale, 10^2 to 10^5 S/m) for electrical conductivity (green and black curves). Axis ticks at x=[300,400,500,600,700] K. Legend in upper right. Three series overlap around 400-450 K.

digitization.description: sequential, prose-style instructions for digitizing the data from this figure. May include extraction order, recommended approach (automated tool vs. manual), specific handling of overlapping data regions, axis discontinuities (broken axes), dual-axis assignments, points to ignore (legends, tick labels), and any other practical guidance. Best practices for writing this field are evolving and will be refined based on curator experience ^-^
digitization.examples: Trace red circles first (5 points, no overlap, single y-axis). For 380-420 K range where blue and green overlap, use linear interpolation between the cleaner regions on either side. Caution: legend in upper right occupies coordinates approximately (700, 50) to (950, 180); exclude this region from automated tracing. Tick labels along axes may also be misread as data points by automated tools.

graphs.description: graphs contained in this figure; subfigure structure (a, b, c, ...) is recorded inside each graph via 'subfigure id' rather than as a separate hierarchy level, keeping the structure flat ^-^

comments.examples: image quality is poor in the published PDF; supplementary version used for digitization

graph.description: a single graph appearing within a figure or subfigure ^-^

graph local id.description: local identifier for this graph within the JSON document. Format: 'g-NNN' ^-^

Starrydata figure id.description: Starrydata figure id (integer) corresponding to this graph; Starrydata's 'figure' granularity matches this schema's 'graph' level (one x-y plot per Starrydata figure record). Optional, populated after registration ^-^

graph name.description: name of the graph as printed in the publication (e.g. 'Fig. 2a'). For human readability and consistency verification ^-^

subfigure label.description: label of the subfigure containing this graph (e.g. 'a', 'b', 'inset'); leave empty if no subfigure structure ^-^

caption summary.description: short summary of the caption text relevant to this graph (may be the subfigure caption or part of the figure caption) ^-^

samples.description: list of sample local ids (from publication.samples[].sample local id) plotted in this graph ^-^

structure.description: brief structural description of the graph; a short summary of the visual structure (panels, axes, types, data series count). Suitable for one-glance overview ^-^
structure.examples: two-panel scatter plot with shared x-axis; single panel line graph; table with 4 columns and 12 rows

description.description: detailed objective textual description of the graph, sufficient for accessibility (visually impaired users) and for readers to reconstruct the underlying data without seeing the original image. Should include scale bars, axis structures, axis ticks, color coding, marker types, line styles, areas of overlap, axis discontinuities (broken axes), legend position, and any other visual details that affect interpretation. This field describes only what is objectively present, not how to extract data from it ^-^
description.examples: Plot has dual y-axes. Left y-axis (linear, 0-200 mu V/K) for Seebeck coefficient (red and blue curves). Right y-axis (log scale, 10^2 to 10^5 S/m) for electrical conductivity (green and black curves). Axis ticks at x=[300,400,500,600,700] K. Legend in upper right. Three series overlap around 400-450 K.

digitization.description: sequential, prose-style instructions for digitizing the data from this graph. May include extraction order, recommended approach (automated tool vs. manual), specific handling of overlapping data regions, axis discontinuities (broken axes), dual-axis assignments, points to ignore (legends, tick labels), and any other practical guidance. Best practices for writing this field are evolving and will be refined based on curator experience ^-^
digitization.examples: Trace red circles first (5 points, no overlap, single y-axis). For 380-420 K range where blue and green overlap, use linear interpolation between the cleaner regions on either side. Caution: legend in upper right occupies coordinates approximately (700, 50) to (950, 180); exclude this region from automated tracing. Tick labels along axes may also be misread as data points by automated tools.

axis.description: specification of one axis of a graph extracted from a figure, including the unit conversion needed to map the axis values into SI base units ^-^

quantity.description: physical quantity displayed on this axis. Always an object with 'term' (human-readable name) and 'ref' (JSON Pointer to a KMDS property vocabulary node when known). For data converted from external sources without measurement context (e.g. Starrydata), ref may be inferred to the most likely property node, with the inference recorded in the per-paper inference log ^-^

ref.description: JSON Pointer to a KMDS property vocabulary node when known; pattern starts with '#/properties/' ^-^

unit.description: unit as printed on the axis, in TeX notation as used throughout KMDS (e.g. 'mu V K^{-1}', 'W m^{-1} K^{-1}') ^-^

reference ticks.description: a small set of tick labels to anchor the axis range; useful when reconstructing data from figure images ^-^

SI conversion.description: explicit formula to convert printed values into SI base units; use 'n/a' when not applicable. Example: 'value * 1e-6' for mu V to V ^-^

comments.description: free-form notes about the axis, useful for non-standard axis configurations such as twin axes, broken axes, reciprocal scales for Arrhenius plots, or any other special considerations ^-^
comments.examples: secondary y-axis on the right shows resistivity; reciprocal temperature scale 1000/T used

table.description: a table extracted from the source publication, preserving headers and rows ^-^

table local id.description: local identifier for this table within the JSON document. Format: 't-NNN' ^-^

Starrydata table id.description: Starrydata table id (integer); reserved for future Starrydata extension when a dedicated table table is added. Currently Starrydata stores tables as figures, but this field anticipates the proper table support ^-^

table name.description: name of the table as printed in the publication (e.g. 'Table 1', 'Table S2'); for human readability and consistency verification ^-^

caption summary.description: short summary of the table caption ^-^

rows.description: rows of the table; each row is an array of cell values aligned with 'headers' ^-^

samples.description: list of sample local ids reported in this table ^-^

structure.description: brief structural description of the table; a short summary of the visual structure (panels, axes, types, data series count). Suitable for one-glance overview ^-^
structure.examples: two-panel scatter plot with shared x-axis; single panel line graph; table with 4 columns and 12 rows

description.description: detailed objective textual description of the table, sufficient for accessibility (visually impaired users) and for readers to reconstruct the underlying data without seeing the original image. Should include scale bars, axis structures, axis ticks, color coding, marker types, line styles, areas of overlap, axis discontinuities (broken axes), legend position, and any other visual details that affect interpretation. This field describes only what is objectively present, not how to extract data from it ^-^
description.examples: Plot has dual y-axes. Left y-axis (linear, 0-200 mu V/K) for Seebeck coefficient (red and blue curves). Right y-axis (log scale, 10^2 to 10^5 S/m) for electrical conductivity (green and black curves). Axis ticks at x=[300,400,500,600,700] K. Legend in upper right. Three series overlap around 400-450 K.

digitization.description: sequential, prose-style instructions for digitizing the data from this table. May include extraction order, recommended approach (automated tool vs. manual), specific handling of overlapping data regions, axis discontinuities (broken axes), dual-axis assignments, points to ignore (legends, tick labels), and any other practical guidance. Best practices for writing this field are evolving and will be refined based on curator experience ^-^
digitization.examples: Trace red circles first (5 points, no overlap, single y-axis). For 380-420 K range where blue and green overlap, use linear interpolation between the cleaner regions on either side. Caution: legend in upper right occupies coordinates approximately (700, 50) to (950, 180); exclude this region from automated tracing. Tick labels along axes may also be misread as data points by automated tools.

passage.description: a passage of body text from the source publication that contains data referenced by this dataset; serves as a peer to figures and tables, recording text-only data sources (e.g. numeric values stated in the Results or Discussion sections, methods conditions, or other narrative descriptions). The internal structure of the passage's data is intentionally not modeled here; downstream data-management systems (e.g. Starrydata-aligned schemas) define how individual values within the passage are represented and linked back to this entry ^-^

passage local id.description: local identifier for this passage within the JSON document. Format: 'p-NNN' ^-^

Starrydata passage id.description: Starrydata passage id (integer); reserved for future Starrydata extension. Currently Starrydata does not have a dedicated table for body-text passages ^-^

passage name.description: human-readable name of the passage (e.g. 'Abstract - peak ZT statement', 'Methods - synthesis') ^-^
passage name.examples: Abstract - peak ZT statement; Methods - synthesis

section.description: section of the publication where the passage appears ^-^
section.examples: abstract; introduction; methods; results; discussion; conclusions; supplementary

text.description: verbatim or summarized text of the passage as relevant to the data being recorded ^-^
text.examples: The Seebeck coefficient of BT-fine reached 230 mu V/K at 450 K, the highest among the three samples studied

samples.description: list of sample local ids referenced in this passage ^-^

author.description: an author of the publication. Combines structured name fields (given/family) with international identifiers (ORCID, Scopus author id), affiliations (allowing multiple), and a roles array describing the author's role(s) in the publication (first, last, corresponding, etc.) ^-^

given name.description: given name(s) of the author (first name and middle names) ^-^

ORCID.description: ORCID identifier of the author in plain ID form (without the URL prefix); ORCID is the international standard researcher identifier ^-^

affiliations.description: list of institutional affiliations of the author. Researchers may have multiple affiliations (cross appointments, visiting positions, etc.) ^-^

email address.description: email address of the author, as published in the paper ^-^

roles.description: list of roles this author plays in the publication. Common values include 'first' (first author), 'last' (last author, often the senior PI), 'corresponding' (corresponding author), 'co-first' (co-first author), 'co-last', 'co-corresponding', and 'equal contribution'. An author may have multiple roles (e.g. both 'first' and 'corresponding') ^-^

affiliation.description: an institutional affiliation of an author. Includes the institution name, country, and optionally the ROR (Research Organization Registry) URL for unambiguous identification ^-^

name.description: institutional name as written in the publication ^-^
name.examples: National Institute for Materials Science; University of Tokyo; RIKEN

ROR.description: Research Organization Registry URL of the institution; the official ROR identifier in URL form. ROR is an open, community-led registry of research organization identifiers ^-^

journal.description: the journal in which the publication appeared. Includes the full journal name, ISO 4 standard abbreviation (with periods), and initials, plus standard identifiers (ISSN, eISSN) and the publisher ^-^

name.examples: Materials Transactions; Nature; Journal of the American Chemical Society

abbreviation.description: ISO 4 standard journal abbreviation, with periods. This is the most widely used abbreviation form in materials and chemistry literature (e.g. Nature, Science, ACS, RSC use this form). Vancouver-style abbreviations (no periods) can be derived by removing periods ^-^
abbreviation.examples: Mater. Trans.; J. Am. Chem. Soc.; Phys. Rev. B

initials.description: compact initials form of the journal name, useful for graph legends and very compact citations ^-^

ISSN.description: International Standard Serial Number (print version), in hyphenated 4-4 format ^-^

eISSN.description: ISSN of the electronic version, in hyphenated 4-4 format ^-^

publisher.examples: Elsevier; Springer Nature; The Japan Institute of Metals and Materials

Scopus source id.description: Scopus source identifier of the journal (numeric string) ^-^

citation.description: structured citation block providing both the constituent parts of the bibliographic citation and ready-to-use formatted citation strings in multiple styles. The 'authors' sub-block holds 4 alternative author name formats (G. Family / Family G. / Given Family / Family, Given) and 'journal' holds 3 forms (name / abbreviation / initials), allowing users to assemble any custom citation style by combining parts. The 'format' sub-block provides commonly-used preformatted citations ^-^

authors.description: author name lists in 4 alternative formats. Field names use 'G.' as shorthand for given-name initial (e.g. Yukari -> Y.). All 4 lists contain the same authors in the same order, just differently formatted, so users can pick the format matching their target citation style ^-^

G. Family.description: given-name initial followed by family name, space-separated. Example: 'Y. Katsura' ^-^

Family G..description: family name followed by given-name initial, space-separated. Example: 'Katsura Y.' ^-^

Given Family.description: full given name followed by family name, space-separated. Example: 'Yukari Katsura' ^-^

Family, Given.description: family name, comma, full given name. Example: 'Katsura, Yukari' ^-^

journal.description: journal name in 3 alternative forms (full name, ISO abbreviation with periods, initials) ^-^

abbreviation.examples: Mater. Trans.; J. Am. Chem. Soc.

first page.description: first page number as a string ^-^

last page.description: last page number as a string ^-^

article number.description: article number for journals using article-number-based pagination (e.g. Nature Communications, Scientific Reports). When article number is not assigned but page range is needed in this field, use single-hyphen page range string (e.g. '1215-1218'). Use null when neither applies ^-^

DOI.description: Digital Object Identifier of the publication ^-^

format.description: ready-to-use formatted citation strings in multiple styles. Field order: lowercase original-style names (key, legend, legend etal, inline) come first, ordered roughly machine-use to human-use; followed by capitalized established style names in alphabetical order (APA, BibTeX, IEEE, Nature, Science, Vancouver). New styles can be added; lowercase original styles take precedence in ordering ^-^

key.description: BibTeX-style citation key, format 'familylastname + year + title-first-word'. Useful for filenames and machine processing ^-^

legend.description: shortest human-readable label, family name + year. Designed for graph legends and slide annotations ^-^

legend etal.description: graph-legend style with et al. for multi-author publications. Single-author papers use 'Family year'; two-author papers use 'Family1 & Family2 year'; three or more authors use 'Family et al. year' ^-^
legend etal.examples: Kumagai et al. 2014; Kumagai & Kurosaki 2014; Smith 2020

inline.description: smallest acceptable inline citation including journal initials, volume, first page, year. Format follows Phys. Rev. style: 'Family et al., Initials Volume, FirstPage (Year)' ^-^

Vancouver.description: Vancouver style (medical/biomedical, no periods in journal abbreviation) ^-^
